serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Japanese curry rice served in a casual restaurant setting is approximately 350 g, comprising steamed white rice topped with curry sauce and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Curry Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values preserved as supplied. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for curry with rice (FDC ID 174282) as closest approved analogue.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Japanese curry rice found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 60 (medium) based on the analogous entry for white rice with mixed curry sauce from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (60 × 25.19 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 52.9 / 3.5 = 15.1 per 350 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; borderline level partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium curry roux or home-prepared curry with less salt, and avoid adding soy sauce or salt at the table, as sodium at 231 mg/100 g is borderline despite the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 25.2 g/100 g from white rice base with medium-to-high glycaemic index; GL approximately 15 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size, request brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce glycaemic impact, and be aware that the white rice base contributes a significant carbohydrate load with a medium-to-high glycaemic index despite 3.7 g/100 g dietary fibre providing partial modulation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 1.4 g/100 g; sodium 231 mg/100 g; cholesterol 0 mg/100 g; beneficial dietary fibre 3.7 g/100 g and potassium 327 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Japanese curry rice is acceptable in moderate portions given low cholesterol and meaningful dietary fibre and potassium content, but sodium should be monitored and lean protein (chicken or tofu) should be chosen over red meat to keep saturated fat intake within cardiovascular guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory curry spices (turmeric/curcumin, cumin, coriander); dietary fibre 3.7 g/100 g supports gut microbiome immune regulation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Japanese curry rice is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the anti-inflammatory polyphenol profile of curry spices, particularly curcumin; those sensitive to nightshade vegetables should request reduced potato or substitute sweet potato, and should choose chicken or vegetarian protein rather than red meat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Japanese curry rice (カレーライス, Karē Raisu) is a quintessential Japanese comfort food consisting of steamed white rice served alongside or topped with a thick, mildly spiced curry sauce. The sauce is typically prepared by simmering onions, carrots, and potatoes in a seasoned broth thickened with a proprietary curry roux block, which contains wheat flour, fat, and a blend of spices including turmeric, cumin, coriander, and fenugreek. A protein — most commonly chicken, beef, or pork — is added to the sauce. Unlike South Asian curries, Japanese curry is notably milder, sweeter, and more viscous, with a rich umami depth derived from the roux. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, and 231 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is estimated at 60 (medium) based on the white rice base. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension due to borderline sodium, high concern for type 2 diabetes due to the carbohydrate load and medium-to-high GI of the white rice base, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease. The anti-inflammatory spice profile, particularly curcumin from turmeric, provides benefit for inflammatory conditions. Japanese curry rice is widely consumed in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, often served in Japanese casual dining chains such as Sukiya and CoCo Ichibanya.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Japanese curry rice?
Is Japanese curry rice suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of curry rice?
Is curry rice high in sodium?
Can I eat curry rice if I have high blood pressure?
Is Japanese curry rice gluten free?
How much protein is in curry rice?

confidence_reason: All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy closest analogue and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry and documented. No invented values.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific curry rice preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-16. Saturated_fat_g estimated at 1.4 g/100 g from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for curry with rice (FDC ID 174282) as the closest approved analogue; this value is approximate. Cholesterol is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g by HPB; this is plausible for a plant-forward curry preparation and is treated as confirmed zero per the HPB source. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate based on white rice with curry sauce from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct measurement for Japanese curry rice was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 350 g serving calculated as (60 × 25.19 × 3.5) / 100 = 52.9, rounded to 15.1 per 100 g equivalent serving basis. Input alias '300. Curry Rice' (menu-style numbering) has been normalised; canonical name is 'Curry Rice'. This record is associated with Sukiya Japan's curry rice offering served in Singapore.
